evidence.title: Management of Acute Pain from Non–Low Back Musculoskeletal Injuries: Guidelines from AAFP and ACP | AAFP
evidence.snippet: Topical nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are the primary recommended treatment, being among the most effective medications and the only to improve all measured outcomes. Topical NSAIDs improve pain within two hours, provide sustained relief over one week, and are the most effective intervention for improving physical function. They also lead to symptom relief and patient satisfaction more than oral NSAIDs. No adverse effects are more common with topical NSAIDs than with placebo. The effectiveness of topical NSAIDs with menthol gel is similar to that of topical NSAIDs alone.

evidence.title: Taking care of your back at home: MedlinePlus Medical Encyclopedia
evidence.snippet: Improve your posture Strengthen your back and abdomen, and improve flexibility Lose weight Avoid falls A complete exercise program should include aerobic activity such as walking, swimming, or riding a stationary bicycle. It should also include stretching and strength training. Follow the instructions of your provider or physical therapist.

evidence.title: Medicines for back pain: MedlinePlus Medical Encyclopedia
evidence.snippet: Updated by: C. Benjamin Ma, MD, Professor, Chief, Sports Medicine and Shoulder Service, UCSF Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, San Francisco, CA. Also reviewed by David C. Dugdale, MD, Medical Director, Brenda Conaway, Editorial Director, and the A.D.A.M. Editorial team.

note_text:
S: The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of dull aching pain localized to the left side of the lower rib cage. The pain improves with good posture and worsens when leaning on the area while sitting. There is no history of trauma, fever, chills, weight loss, respiratory symptoms, neurological symptoms, or urinary complaints. She has tried acetaminophen (Tylenol) without significant relief. There is no history of osteoporosis, cancer, or recent infections.

O: Phone consultation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: The working diagnosis is musculoskeletal pain likely due to strain or minor soft tissue injury in the left lower rib cage area.
Differential:
1. Musculoskeletal strain or soft tissue injury
2. Costochondritis
3. Intercostal nerve irritation
4. Early presentation of rib fracture or underlying pathology (less likely given no trauma or systemic symptoms)

P: First-line medication: Naproxen 500 mg PO twice daily with food for 7 days
Alternatives: Diclofenac 50 mg PO three times daily with food for 7 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing), Meloxicam 15 mg PO once daily for 7 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
Medication safety considerations: Use with caution in patients with gastrointestinal risk factors; advise taking with food to minimize GI irritation. Monitor for any signs of adverse effects such as abdominal pain or bleeding.
Return precautions: Advise patient to seek immediate care if she develops fever, worsening pain, neurological symptoms (numbness, weakness), chest pain, shortness of breath, or any new systemic symptoms.
Testing: Not indicated at this time given absence of red flags; consider imaging if symptoms persist beyond 6 weeks or worsen despite treatment.
